one_line_takeaway: FinSec is a four-layer (AML/SAR pattern matching + generative-rollout deferred-risk simulation + LLM semantic-safety judgment + weighted fusion) detection framework for multi-turn financial-agent dialogues that raises F1 from 76.48% (R-Judge/O3 baseline) to 90.13% and cuts ASR to 9.09% on the R-Judge financial subset, with an explicit ablation showing that folding adversarial reasoning into a single prompt (FinO3adv) underperforms separating it into its own architectural layer.
key_finding: On the R-Judge financial-agent dialogue subset (Table II), FinSec achieves an overall Utility F1 of 90.13%, versus 76.48% for the R-Judge/O3 zero-shot baseline, 76.48% for FinO3, and 82.94% for FinO3adv. Per-category F1: Injection Attack F1 rises from 72.97% (baseline) to 94.55% (FinSec); Unintended Attack F1 rises from 80.00% (baseline) to 85.71% (FinSec), notably higher than the intermediate FinO3 (73.68%) and FinO3adv (81.82%) variants. The paper reports FinSec's ASR is reduced to 9.09% and its AUPRC (structured) increases to 0.9189, described as an approximate 9.7% gain over 'general frameworks' (the specific compared framework/baseline value for this delta is not stated in the extracted text). The composite risk-adjusted score RFinSec is reported as 0.9098, and the paper separately claims a '12% overall performance improvement' over the R-Judge baseline on this composite metric. A Pareto-frontier plot (Fig. 8) is used to argue FinSec exceeds the injection-vs-unintended-risk trade-off boundary formed by baseline LLMs including Claude Opus 4 and Gemini 2.5 Pro. The FinO3adv ablation is presented as a negative finding: folding adversarial-verification instructions directly into a single prompt layer did not improve, and sometimes degraded (e.g., unintended-risk specificity dropping to 33.33% for FinO3adv), detection versus separating adversarial reasoning into its own architectural layer (motivating FinSec's Layer 2 design).
core_contribution: The paper proposes FinSec, a four-layer detection framework for identifying unsafe/risky multi-turn dialogues between users and LLM-based financial agents. Layer 1 (Suspicious Behavior Pattern Detection) applies AML/SAR-style 'red flag' pattern matching over sliding dialogue windows, combining keyword hit rate, semantic similarity, and sequence-order consistency into a pattern score M_k. Layer 2 (Deferred Risk Assessment and Adversarial Rollout) estimates a deferred-risk score from suspicion, user profile, and history features, and when this exceeds a threshold triggers a generative rollout that samples N simulated future dialogue continuations (via a two-stage few-shot LLM prompt) to compute a worst-case rollout risk score. Layer 3 (Semantic Safety Assessment) uses an LLM as a semantic discriminator over the full multi-turn conversation plus few-shot financial-security examples, first generating a reasoning trace and then a discrete Safe/Unsafe label. Layer 4 (Risk Fusion) linearly combines the three layers' scores with weights (semantic layer weighted highest at gamma=0.75, others 0.10-0.15) to produce a final risk score and binary block/allow decision. The authors also report an intermediate baseline they call FinO3/FinO3adv (prompt-engineered O3 with adversarial-structure prompting) as a precursor before arriving at the full multi-layer FinSec design, and find that simply adding adversarial-verification text into a single prompt layer does not help and can hurt performance versus restructuring adversarial reasoning into a separate architectural layer.
threat_model: The paper targets user-issued natural-language inputs to LLM-based financial agents (e.g., wealth-management assistants, transaction/compliance chatbots) that can trigger real financial operations such as transfers or balance changes. Two attacker-relevant behaviors are modeled: (1) 'injection attacks' -- adversarial or malicious instructions intended to induce unsafe agent actions (e.g., prompt injection, social engineering, encoding-based evasion, stepwise/roundabout request decomposition designed to evade single-turn detection); and (2) 'unintended risks' -- risky agent outputs/actions not necessarily from a deliberate attacker but arising from ordinary financial dialogue (e.g., over-eager compliance, hallucinated advice). Risk is explicitly modeled as accumulating and manifesting with delay across multiple dialogue turns rather than being detectable in a single turn, motivated by financial-compliance evasion patterns (structuring, gradual extraction, rapid escalation, circular flow, timing anomaly, social engineering) drawn from AML/SAR/KYC/FATF regulatory concepts. The defender is a monitoring layer sitting alongside/after the financial agent, not the agent's core model itself.
limitations: Limitations stated by the paper itself (Conclusions section): (1) under long or complex prompts / long-sequence inputs, LLM output quality degrades, reducing detection accuracy, and this effect worsens as the overall architecture grows more complex; the authors flag this as needing dedicated optimization for long-sequence financial risk prompts. (2) The paper acknowledges that real-world major financial losses are often driven specifically by intentional injection attacks, and states the current injection-attack categorization needs further refinement with more targeted detection/defense mechanisms, left to future work. Not explicitly discussed by the authors, but evident from the paper: several equations/derivations are marked '[need ref]' or '[need references]' in-line (e.g., the citation for adversarial-analysis-for-robustness assumption, and for user-profile/historical-semantic-manipulation modeling in Layer 2), suggesting the manuscript is an unfinished/pre-camera-ready draft; the evaluation is confined to the R-Judge financial subset only, so generalization to other financial-agent deployments or datasets is untested in the paper; the paper does not report the size (N examples) of the R-Judge financial subset, and its 'general frameworks' AUPRC comparison baseline is referenced only qualitatively (~9.7% gain) without naming the specific compared system in the paper.

datasets_used: R-Judge (financial-domain subset): agent-risk benchmark with 5 domain classifications and 27 manually labeled risk scenarios, used as both the base-model selection benchmark and the FinSec evaluation set
quantitative_results: Base-model screening (10 LLMs, zero-shot CoT on R-Judge financial data): O3 overall F1 76.48%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 76.40%, DeepSeek Chat injection-defense F1 79.41%, Claude Opus 4 unintended-risk recall 100.00% / specificity 48.78%, GPT-O3 unintended-risk specificity 75.61%

Table I (R-Judge(O3) vs FinO3 variants): R-Judge(O3) All F1 76.49%, Injection F1 80.00% (Recall 66.67%, Spec 100.00%), Unintended F1 72.97% (Recall 100.00%, Spec 75.61%); FinO3 (Zero-shot) All F1 82.05%; FinO3 (Few-shot) All F1 83.27%, Injection F1 73.68% (Recall 63.64%, Spec 66.67%), Unintended F1 92.86% (Recall 100.00%, Spec 95.06%); FinO3adv All F1 82.85%, Injection F1 83.87% (Recall 100.00%, Spec 87.65%), Unintended F1 81.82% (Recall 81.82%, Spec 33.33%)

Table II (final comparison): O3/R-Judge Utility F1 76.48%, Injection F1 72.97%, Unintended F1 80.00%; FinO3 Utility F1 76.48%, Injection F1 83.27%, Unintended F1 73.68%; FinO3adv Utility F1 82.94%, Injection F1 83.87%, Unintended F1 81.82%; FinSec Utility F1 90.13%, Injection F1 94.55%, Unintended F1 85.71%

FinSec overall: ASR reduced to 9.09%; AUPRC (structured) = 0.9189 (~9.7% gain over 'general frameworks', comparator not named in extracted text); composite RFinSec score = 0.9098; ~12% overall composite-score improvement over R-Judge baseline claimed in text

Layer 2 frequency-anomaly weight ablation: weight configuration w=0.2 selected as optimal across Discrimination, Balance, Robustness, Scalability dimensions (Figs. 4-6), with the composite score peaking at w=0.2 and remaining high over w=0.15-0.3
baselines_compared: R-Judge(O3): zero-shot-CoT prompted O3 as the un-modified baseline detector
FinO3 (zero-shot and few-shot variants): prompt-optimized O3 without the multi-layer architecture
FinO3adv: FinO3 with adversarial-verification structure folded into the single prompt (ablation showing this design choice underperforms FinSec's separated-layer approach)
10-model LLM screening panel referenced in Fig. 3 (includes at least O3, Gemini 2.5 Pro, DeepSeek Chat, Claude Opus 4, GPT-O3 by name in the extracted text)
